summary: When I started working for the state, my role was with the Gaming Commission, but I actually served as the public information officer and press spokesman for both the Gaming Commission and the Gaming Control Board. The Gaming Control Board, chaired by Edward A. Olsen, was the administrative body responsible for license investigations, enforcement, and daily administration of gaming laws. The board members were full-time employees, and they handled the tough work of regulating gaming in Nevada, especially during Governor Grant Sawyer's administration in the 1960s.

Edward A. Olsen was the chairman of the Gaming Control Board when I worked for the state. He held one of the most important positions in Nevada government and was the right man to enforce Governor Grant Sawyer's tough gaming control policies. I reported primarily to Ed, and he led the board with a firm hand during a critical time for gaming regulation. His leadership was central to the board's efforts to keep the mob out of Nevada casinos.

Governor Grant Sawyer was elected on a platform of tough gaming control policies, which shaped much of the work done by the Gaming Control Board and Commission during my time. His administration demanded strict oversight of the gaming industry to keep out organized crime. The board, led by Edward A. Olsen, was aligned with Sawyer's vision, and this period saw significant enforcement actions, including the Sinatra license revocation case.

Ned Turner was one of the members of the Gaming Control Board alongside Edward A. Olsen and Butch Leypoldt. Before joining the board, Ned had been a clerk of the Nevada Supreme Court, which gave him a solid legal background. His experience contributed to the board's work in enforcing gaming laws during a time when the state was cracking down on illegal gambling and organized crime.

The Nevada Supreme Court was connected to the Gaming Control Board through Ned Turner, who had served as a clerk there before joining the board. This link underscored the legal foundation behind gaming regulation in Nevada. The court's decisions and the board's enforcement actions, such as in the Sinatra license revocation case, were part of the state's efforts to maintain integrity in gaming.

Butch Leypoldt was a member of the Gaming Control Board during my time with the state. He had previously served as Clark County sheriff, which brought valuable law enforcement experience to the board. Along with Edward A. Olsen and Ned Turner, Butch helped oversee the daily administration of gaming laws and enforcement in Nevada.

Milton Keefer was the chairman of the Gaming Commission throughout my tenure, based in Las Vegas. He was a private practice attorney and a strong, demanding leader who closely scrutinized the Gaming Control Board's recommendations. Keefer was instrumental in key decisions like the Sinatra license revocation case and was backed firmly by James Hotchkiss in opposing mob influence in Nevada gaming.

Las Vegas was the central hub for much of the gaming regulation work during my time with the state. Milton Keefer, the chairman of the Gaming Commission, practiced law there, and many of the commission's members, including James Hotchkiss, were based in Vegas. The city was the frontline in the fight against organized crime in casinos, especially under Governor Grant Sawyer's administration in the 1960s.

James Hotchkiss was a member of the Gaming Commission from Las Vegas and ran an armored car service. He and Milton Keefer shared similar backgrounds as former FBI agents, which made them strong opponents of mob influence in Nevada gaming. Jim was a staunch supporter of the Black Book concept and played a key role in enforcement actions like the Sinatra license revocation case.

Reno was represented on the Gaming Commission by two members during my time: F. E. 'Pete' Walters, a realtor, and Pete Petersen, a former postmaster. While Pete Walters was knowledgeable and kept up with gaming issues, Pete Petersen was more of a political appointee and less active in leadership. Reno's representation helped balance the commission's statewide perspective on gaming regulation.

F. E. 'Pete' Walters was a realtor from Reno and a member of the Gaming Commission. He was well-informed and kept up with the issues facing gaming regulation. I viewed him as a good and effective member of the commission during the time I worked with the state.

Pete Petersen was a former postmaster from Reno and served on the Gaming Commission. He was probably a political appointee and, while a good fellow, he didn't exercise much leadership that I recall. His role was more passive compared to other commission members during the 1960s.

Smith Valley was home to Norman Brown, a rancher and member of the Gaming Commission. Norman was a pillar of integrity and deeply cared about Nevada's welfare. Sometimes he missed meetings because he was busy driving his cattle between Smith Valley and Bridgeport, but he was a strong advocate for strong gambling control and keeping the mob out.

Norman Brown was a rancher from Smith Valley and a member of the Gaming Commission. He was a man of great integrity who cared deeply about Nevada and its gaming industry. Even though he sometimes missed meetings due to cattle drives between Smith Valley and Bridgeport, I admired him greatly and arranged speaking appearances for him to promote strong gaming control.

The Sinatra license revocation case was a significant enforcement action during my time with the Gaming Commission and Board. Milton Keefer, as chairman of the commission, and James Hotchkiss strongly supported the decision to revoke Frank Sinatra's gaming license as part of the effort to keep organized crime out of Nevada casinos. This case exemplified the tough stance Governor Grant Sawyer's administration took on gaming control in the 1960s.

The Black Book was a key concept embraced by the Gaming Commission members like Milton Keefer and James Hotchkiss. It was a list used to weed out undesirable individuals, especially those connected to organized crime, from the gaming industry. This approach was central to the commission's efforts to maintain integrity and keep the mob out of Nevada casinos during the 1960s.

Bob Cahill was the executive secretary of the Tax Commission in the 1950s and was essentially the state's first line of defense in gaming control before the Gaming Control Board and Commission were created. He attempted background investigations and was a towering figure of his time. His work paved the way for the tougher gaming laws that came later under Governor Grant Sawyer.

Before the creation of the Gaming Control Board and Commission, the Tax Commission was the primary body responsible for gaming control in Nevada. Bob Cahill, the executive secretary, was the main enforcer, using tax laws to regulate licensees. However, their authority was limited compared to the later, more robust gaming control bodies established in the 1960s.

Bridgeport, California, was part of Norman Brown's cattle driving route. As a rancher from Smith Valley, Norman sometimes missed Gaming Commission meetings because he was busy driving his cattle between Smith Valley and Bridgeport. This showed how his ranching responsibilities sometimes took precedence over his commission duties.
